s: In Japanese mythology, the islands of Japan were created when the gods Izanagi and Izanami stirred the ocean with a jeweled spear — the drops that fell from its tip became the first island.
q: In Japanese creation mythology, how were the islands of Japan formed?
a: Drops from a jeweled spear stirring the ocean
d: The gods threw a jeweled stone into the ocean
Two great whales swam together and created land
A sacred spider wove the islands from its web
The sun god descended and formed islands with fire
Seven dragons coiled together to form the land
A giant god lay down and his body became the islands
e: The primordial couple stood on the Floating Bridge of Heaven and churned the chaotic sea below. The brine that dripped from the Amenonuhoko spear piled up to form Onogoro-shima, the self-forming island, where they descended to create the rest of Japan.

s: Eris, the Greek goddess of discord, started the Trojan War by tossing a golden apple labeled 'For the Fairest' into a party she was not invited to — three goddesses then fought over it.
q: What did Eris throw into a party to start the chain of events leading to the Trojan War?
a: A golden apple inscribed 'For the Fairest'
e: Hera, Athena, and Aphrodite each claimed the Apple of Discord. Paris of Troy was chosen to judge, and each goddess offered him a bribe. He chose Aphrodite's offer — the most beautiful woman in the world — which was Helen of Sparta, triggering the war.

s: The Norse god Heimdall needs less sleep than a bird, can hear grass growing, and can see for 300 miles — all so he can guard the rainbow bridge Bifrost against the giants.
q: Which Norse god can hear grass growing and see for 300 miles because he guards the rainbow bridge Bifrost?
a: Heimdall
e: Heimdall's senses are supernaturally enhanced because he serves as the eternal watchman of Asgard. At Ragnarok, he will blow the Gjallarhorn to signal the final battle, then die in mutual combat with Loki.

s: In Inuit mythology, the Northern Lights are the spirits of the dead playing football with a walrus skull across the arctic sky.
q: In one Inuit myth, what are the spirits of the dead doing when they create the Northern Lights?
a: Playing football with a walrus skull
d: Shaking hands with spirits across the sky
Ghosts telling stories of the hunt
Shamans conducting rituals in the heavens
e: Different Inuit groups have varying aurora legends. Some say the lights are spirits playing ball; others say they are torches held by spirits guiding newly departed souls. Whistling at the aurora was believed to bring the spirits dangerously close.

s: Hephaestus, the Greek god of blacksmithing, built golden robot servants to help him in his forge — making him mythology's first inventor of artificial intelligence.
q: Which Greek god created golden robotic servants that could think and speak — mythology's first AI?
a: Hephaestus
e: Homer's Iliad describes Hephaestus creating golden maidens (automata) that could think, speak, and work. He also built Talos, a bronze giant who patrolled Crete, and self-moving tables that rolled to feasts on their own.

s: Asclepius, the Greek god of medicine, was so skilled at healing that he could resurrect the dead — Zeus killed him with a thunderbolt because Hades complained about losing customers.
q: Why did Zeus kill Asclepius, the Greek god of medicine?
a: Hades complained that Asclepius was resurrecting the dead
d: He broke his oath to the gods
He fell in love with a mortal and ignored his duties
Apollo grew jealous of his healing powers
He tried to steal from Hephaestus
He refused to heal a god's favorite
e: Asclepius learned to revive the dead using Gorgon blood given to him by Athena. His Rod of Asclepius — a staff with a single serpent — remains the symbol of medicine worldwide today.

s: In Cherokee mythology, the Earth is a floating island suspended from the sky vault by four cords — and when the cords snap, the world will sink back into the primordial ocean.
q: In Cherokee mythology, what suspends the Earth from the sky vault?
a: Four cords
e: The Cherokee believed the earth was formed when a water beetle dove beneath the primordial waters and brought up mud that expanded into land. The Great Buzzard then flew over it, and where its tired wings dipped, mountains and valleys formed.

s: Selene, the Greek moon goddess, fell so deeply in love with the shepherd Endymion that she asked Zeus to put him in an eternal sleep — so she could visit him every night forever without him aging.
q: Why did the Greek moon goddess Selene ask Zeus to put the shepherd Endymion into eternal sleep?
a: So she could visit him every night without him ever aging
d: Because she was jealous of his mortal wife
To punish him for refusing her love
To make him immortal and rule with her
Because he rejected her divine advances
To test his devotion to another goddess
So his soul would remain pure
e: Zeus granted the wish, and Endymion sleeps eternally on Mount Latmos in a cave. Selene visits him nightly as the moon passes overhead, and legends say they had fifty daughters together despite his perpetual slumber.

s: The Churel in South Asian folklore is the ghost of a woman who died during childbirth — she appears beautiful from the front but has backward-facing feet, which is how you identify her.
q: How can you identify a Churel — a dangerous ghost in South Asian folklore?
a: Her feet face backward
d: Eyes on the back of her head
A mouth where her stomach is
Arms twice as long as normal
e: Churels are found in Hindu, Muslim, and tribal folklore across India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh. They lure young men into forests and drain their life force. Some traditions say placing mustard seeds near a grave prevents a Churel from rising.

s: Anansi stories traveled from West Africa to the Caribbean on slave ships, and in Jamaica they evolved into 'Nancy stories' — one of the most remarkable examples of mythology surviving the Middle Passage.
q: What West African trickster god's stories survived the Middle Passage and became 'Nancy stories' in Jamaica?
a: Anansi
e: The Akan trickster spider Anansi became a symbol of resistance and cleverness for enslaved peoples. His stories of outsmarting powerful oppressors through wit rather than strength carried coded messages about survival and subversion.

s: The Qalupalik in Inuit mythology is a green-skinned sea creature that hides under ice and snatches disobedient children who wander too close to the shoreline.
q: What Inuit mythological creature hides under sea ice to snatch children who wander too close to shore?
a: Qalupalik
e: Qalupalik stories served a practical purpose: keeping children away from dangerous sea ice and frigid Arctic waters. The creature is described as having long hair, long fingernails, and a humming sound that lures curious children closer.

s: In Yoruba mythology, the god Ogun was so grief-stricken after accidentally killing his own people in a battle frenzy that he drove his sword into the earth and sank into the ground, becoming iron ore.
q: In Yoruba mythology, what happened to the god Ogun after he accidentally killed his own people?
a: He drove his sword into the earth and became iron ore
d: He challenged Zeus and was struck down
He transformed into a star in the sky
He was imprisoned in the center of the earth
He became the river that flows through Yoruba lands
He merged with the ocean and became a sea spirit
He ascended to the sky as the sun
e: Ogun is the deity of iron, war, and labor. His transformation into the very substance of his craft makes him one of mythology's most poetic origin stories for a natural resource. Blacksmiths and metalworkers across West Africa honor him as their patron.

s: Persephone ate only six pomegranate seeds in the underworld — and that is why the ancient Greeks said we have six months of winter, one month for each seed she consumed.
q: In Greek mythology, why does Persephone spend six months in the underworld each year?
a: She ate six pomegranate seeds there
e: Hades tricked Persephone into eating the seeds, binding her to the underworld for part of each year. Her mother Demeter, goddess of harvest, causes all plants to wither during Persephone's absence — creating winter.

s: The Hawaiian goddess Pele is believed to still live in Kilauea volcano — and locals leave offerings of gin and ohelo berries at the crater's edge to appease her.
q: Which Hawaiian goddess is believed to live in Kilauea volcano and is still given offerings today?
a: Pele
e: Pele is one of the few mythological figures actively worshipped at a specific geographic location today. Visitors who take lava rocks home often mail them back after experiencing 'Pele's Curse' — a streak of bad luck attributed to the goddess.

s: In Egyptian mythology, the chaos serpent Apophis attacked Ra's sun boat every single night — and every sunrise was proof that Ra had won the nightly battle again.
q: In Egyptian mythology, what happened every night to the sun god Ra's boat?
a: The chaos serpent Apophis attacked it
d: A great whirlpool formed in the sky
Demons tried to steal the sun
A giant spider blocked the sun's path
The moon covered the sky entirely
Ra's boat sank into the deep waters
e: The entire Egyptian priesthood performed rituals each night to help Ra defeat Apophis. The serpent could never be permanently killed, only temporarily subdued. Solar eclipses were believed to be moments when Apophis was winning.

s: The Kelpie in Scottish folklore appears as a beautiful horse by a river — but once you mount it, your hands stick to its hide and it drags you underwater to drown you.
q: What Scottish folklore creature appears as a beautiful horse but drowns anyone who rides it?
a: Kelpie
e: The Kelpie could also appear as a handsome man, identifiable only by the water weeds in his hair. One legend says the only way to tame a Kelpie is to get its bridle — then it must obey you, and its strength equals that of ten horses.

s: Marduk, the chief god of Babylon, defeated the primordial chaos goddess Tiamat by shooting an arrow down her throat while she held it open to swallow him — then he split her body to create heaven and earth.
q: In Babylonian mythology, how did Marduk kill the chaos goddess Tiamat?
a: Shot an arrow down her throat while she tried to swallow him
d: He cut her into pieces and scattered them
He drowned her in the cosmic sea
He bound her with magical chains
He burned her with divine fire
e: The Enuma Elish (Babylonian creation epic) describes Marduk inflating Tiamat's body with wind before the killing blow. Her upper half became the sky, her lower half the earth, her eyes the sources of the Tigris and Euphrates rivers.

s: The Ponaturi in Maori mythology are sea goblins who die instantly if exposed to sunlight — so their enemies defeated them by blocking the windows of their cave and tricking them into thinking it was still night.
q: How were the Ponaturi — sunlight-fearing sea goblins — defeated in Maori mythology?
a: Their cave windows were blocked, tricking them into emerging into daylight
d: They were trapped in a sealed tomb
They were bound by magical chains
They were cast into the sea forever
They were frozen by a winter spell
e: In the legend, the hero Rata's father Wahieroa was killed by the Ponaturi. Rata's relatives sealed the cave at dawn and waited. When the Ponaturi finally emerged, thinking it was dark, the sudden sunlight killed them all instantly.

s: The Cadejo in Central American folklore comes in two forms — a white spirit dog that protects night travelers, and a black spirit dog that leads them to their doom.
q: In Central American folklore, what are the two forms of the spirit dog called the Cadejo?
a: A protective white dog and a deadly black dog
d: A guardian angel and a demon
A young dog and an old dog
A male and a female spirit
A day creature and a night creature
A fire spirit and a water spirit
e: The white Cadejo has been compared to a guardian angel in dog form, while the black Cadejo is associated with the Devil. In some versions, the two dogs fight each other over the traveler's fate, and whichever wins determines whether the person survives the night.

s: Eos, the Greek goddess of the dawn, was cursed by Aphrodite to be eternally boy-crazy — because Eos once had an affair with Aphrodite's lover Ares.
q: Why was the Greek dawn goddess Eos cursed with insatiable desire for mortal men?
a: She had an affair with Aphrodite's lover Ares
d: She drank from Aphrodite's magic fountain
She insulted Aphrodite at a divine banquet
She wore Aphrodite's golden girdle without permission
e: This curse drove Eos to kidnap multiple handsome mortals. Her most famous captive was Tithonus, for whom she requested immortality from Zeus but forgot to ask for eternal youth — so he aged forever, eventually shriveling into a cicada.

s: In Mayan mythology, the gods failed to create humans twice — once with mud (too soft) and once with wood (no soul) — before finally succeeding with corn dough on the third attempt.
q: According to the Mayan Popol Vuh, what material were humans successfully created from on the third attempt?
a: Corn dough
e: The Popol Vuh describes the wood people as functional but soulless, so the gods sent a great flood and animal attacks to destroy them. The surviving wood people became monkeys. The corn dough humans were so perfect that the gods had to cloud their vision to prevent them from becoming too powerful.

s: The Nuckelavee from Orcadian folklore is Scotland's most horrifying monster — a skinless horse-rider fused into one being, with a single giant eye and breath that wilts crops and causes plague.
q: What is unique about the Nuckelavee's physical appearance in Orcadian folklore?
a: It has no skin — its muscles and blood are fully visible
d: It has two heads connected by a single neck
It breathes fire and ice simultaneously
It has six legs and four wings
It can split into two separate creatures
It has a face on its chest instead of its head
e: Unlike most Scottish monsters, the Nuckelavee has no skin — its black blood, muscles, and sinews are completely visible. The only thing that stops it is fresh running water. It emerges from the sea and is held in check by the sea spirit Mither o' the Sea during summer.
